mnemonics: ELECT to COORDINATE: Use Elections for a Coordinator. MUTEX for Agreement: Mutual Exclusion for critical sections. FLP is Impossible: Fischer-Lynch-Paterson proves consensus is impossible with one faulty process.
keyPoints: Coordination often requires electing a leader (e.g., using the Bully or Ring algorithm) to manage resources and make decisions.
Agreement protocols like Consensus and Mutual Exclusion ensure all non-faulty processes decide on the same value or access a resource sequentially.
The FLP Impossibility result is a fundamental limitation: in an asynchronous system, no deterministic consensus protocol can tolerate even a single crash failure.
commonMistakes: Assuming a perfect synchronous network: Many protocols fail in asynchronous systems where there are no timing guarantees, leading to mistakes in understanding the FLP result.
Confusing coordination with agreement: Coordination is about organizing processes (e.g., leader election), while agreement is about getting processes to decide on a common value (e.g., consensus).
Ignoring fault tolerance: Designing algorithms that work only when no processes fail, which is unrealistic in a distributed system.
